retrospective.harder_than_needed: AC-UX-1's Phase A test asserted an exact `hsl(hue, sat%, light%)` substring match against the stub's output. The real @rotheric/visuals engine emits `hsla(hue sat% light% / alpha)` (CSS Color 4 syntax, comma-free) with the hue JITTERED per zone by a scheme-dependent offset around the pinned anchorHue -- so the exact-match assertion would have either been permanently unsatisfiable or silently wrong if ported naively. Confirming this required actually installing and calling the real package (not just reading its README/types) across multiple schemes/anchors/trials to find a safe, empirically-grounded tolerance (45 degrees, ~9x the observed worst case) rather than guessing a number. Separately, wiring the git dependency safely required working out a credential path this sandbox doesn't have by default (no SSH key for github.com) without embedding a token in any committed file -- solved by seeding git's own credential cache out-of-band and letting npm's HTTPS git-dependency resolution run over the (locally cached-credential) transport, which also surfaced npm's own hosted-git-info normalization quirk (package.json rewritten to `github:owner/repo#tag` shorthand; package-lock.json's resolved field recorded as `git+ssh://...` even though the fetch itself went over HTTPS).
retrospective.surprised_by: The real package's per-zone hue is not the exact pinned anchorHue -- it is jittered by a scheme-dependent offset (worst observed ~33 degrees across 6 schemes/8 anchors/5 trials), which the Phase A stub (a hand-rolled placeholder) never modeled since it wrote the pinned hue verbatim. A test suite carried over unchanged from the stub would not fail loudly here -- it would just never see the string it's looking for, silently making the AC-UX-1 'reflects pinned identity' guarantee vacuous the moment it started comparing against real output, unless someone thought to check.
